definitions: Any of several widely distributed evergreen flowering plants, of the genus Osmanthus.
examples: By 1969, the flower district had under cultivation over 13 hectares of rosebeds alone, 10,000 pots of osmanthus, 50,000 pots of magnolias, camellias and jasmine and several hundred thousand pots of chrysanthemums, as well as thousands of pine, pomegranate and iron trees.

(chemistry) An oxidizing agent, especially one used to oxidize the fuel of a rocket or a fuel cell.
examples: BioLab officials told the investigators they had established a permanent fire watch two or three months prior to the incident after strong odors from oxidizers in two storage buildings were detected, according to the CSB report. BioLab also told CSB two employees were on duty for fire watch on September 29.

Both chemicals are solid oxidizers in granular form and have a chlorine odor. […] Plant 12 also stored super sacks of bromochloro-5,5-dimethylimidazolidine-2,4-dione (BCDMH), which is a granular solid oxidizer with a halogen odor that, in addition to chlorine gas and hydrogen chloride, can also release bromine gas and hydrogen bromide upon decomposition.

definitions: present participle and gerund of oxidize
That oxidizes.
(biochemistry) Any enzyme that catalyzes an oxidation - reduction (redox) reaction.
examples: It remains to be seen whether oxidoreductase deficiency is a significant cause of non-syndromic ambiguous genitalia in newborn females and undermasculinized males of unknown diagnosis as assessed by urinary steroid analyses.

definitions: (chemistry) Synonym of redox reaction.
(organic chemistry) Any of a class of organic compounds, of general formula RR'C=NOH, derived from the condensation of an aldehyde (R' = H) or ketone with hydroxylamine.
examples: In contrast, a water-repellent substance, for example, fluoric resin or Teflon, is extremely nonaffinitive with the oily oxime hydrochlorides and has a nature to repel it.
Treatment for novichok exposure would be the same as for other nerve agents, with atropine, diazepam and drugs called oximes.

definitions: (chemistry) A device that measures the quantity of oxygen in something, particularly air in the atmosphere.
examples: The instruments in which the oxigen gas of a determined quantity of air was ascertained, received the name of EUDIOMETERS, because they were considered as measurers of the purity of air. They are however more properly called OXIMETERS.
EUDIO′METER. [...] An instrument by which the quantity of oxygen and nitrogen in atmospheric air is ascertained: an oxymeter.

Recalibration of the oxymeters was done in January 1969 with a standard solution. Oxygen recordings by the oxymeters were as low as 10 ppb and agreed well with the Winkler tests. The Magna oxymeter was used successfully throughout the operation period to monitor the oxygen level in the deaerated brine.

Is it not to be regretted that the term eudiometry and eudiometer should be applied exclusively to the art of determining the quantity of oxygen in the air and the instruments for that purpose? Would not the terms oximetry and oximeter be more appropriate?—Tr[anslator].]

definitions: A plant of species Primula elatior, similar to cowslip (Primula veris) but with larger, pale yellow flowers.
(mineralogy) Containing oxygen.
The tail of an ox or cow.
A cut of meat taken from the tail of a cow or calf; it requires long, slow braising, and is used to prepare oxtail soup.
A flowering plant of the genus Anchusa, especially Anchusa officinalis (true alkanet).
A flowering plant of the genus Picris, especially Picris echioides (bristly oxtongue).
Beef tongue (a foodstuff).
(organic chemistry) A mixture of oxygen and acetylene, which burns at a high temperature and is used for cutting and welding metals.
Using this mixture.
examples: an oxyacetylene torch

definitions: (chemistry) An acid containing oxygen.
(organic chemistry) hydroxybutyric
A kind of cephalic disorder: sometimes the premature closure of the coronal suture plus any other suture, such as the lambdoid; sometimes the premature fusing of all sutures.
(figurative) A condition or environment in which something can thrive.

The chemical element (symbol O) with an atomic number of 8 and relative atomic mass of 15.9994. It is a colorless and odorless gas. Sometimes called elemental oxygen to distinguish it from molecular oxygen.
(loose but very common, even in formal use) Molecular oxygen (O 2), a colorless, odorless gas at room temperature.
(medicine) A mixture of oxygen and other gases, administered to a patient to help them breathe.
An atom of this element.
examples: By molar fraction, calcium oxide contains equal parts calcium and oxygen.
They hoped to starve the terrorists of the oxygen of publicity.
About 21% of the air you breathe is oxygen.
Silence is the oxygen of shame.
Look first at any structure to see if there is a carbon with two oxygens attached. Hemiacetals, hemiketals, acetals, and ketals are all alike in that regard.

definitions: (biochemistry) An enzyme which catalyses biochemical reactions with oxygen.
(transitive, medicine) To give (a patient) oxygen therapy.
To treat or infuse with oxygen.
examples: After we oxygenated the river, the fish returned.

definitions: The process of reacting or treating something with oxygen.
To oxidize.
To oxygenate.
(biochemistry) The form of haemoglobin, loosely combined with oxygen, present in arterial and capillary blood.
(American spelling) Alternative form of oxyhaemoglobin.
(rhetoric) A figure of speech in which two words or phrases with opposing meanings are used together intentionally for effect.
examples: In Oxymoron jarring phrases join And terms opposed in harmony combine.
“Stable inflation is an oxymoron because it means it’s not stable,” Shelton told CNN in a recent interview.

For Theodor Adorno and his colleagues at the Frankfurt School who coined the term, "culture industry" was an oxymoron, intended to set up a critical contrast between the exploitative, repetitive mode of industrial mass production under capitalism and the associations of transformative power and aesthetico-moral transcendence that the concept of culture carried in the 1940s, when it still meant "high" culture.

During the past few years, some 200 class-action suits have been filed against food manufacturers, charging them with misuse of the adjective in marketing such edible oxymorons as “natural” Cheetos Puffs, “all-natural” Sun Chips, “all-natural” Naked Juice, “100 percent all-natural” Tyson chicken nuggets and so forth.

definitions: Excessive acuteness of sight.
(pharmacology) A metabolite of phenylbutazone.
A particular antimuscarinic drug.

(pharmacology) A yellow crystalline broad-spectrum tetracycline antibiotic C 22 H 24 N 2 O 9 that is produced by a soil actinomycete (Streptomyces rimosus) and works by interfering with bacteria 's ability to produce essential proteins.
An oxytocic drug.
(medicine) Serving to promote uterine contractions, thus accelerating childbirth.
(biochemistry) A hormone that stimulates contractions during labor, and then the production of milk; also plays a role in social bonding.
examples: When human beings bond—by hugging, kissing, touching, having sex, eating together, giving massages, or even when singing together or speaking reassuring words— oxytocin brings us “in from the cold,” warming us—quite literally—as it redirects blood flow into hands, feet, chests and cheeks.

Some women describe episodes of intense pleasure akin to orgasm during nursing. It would be interesting to know whether the release of oxytocin during suckling plays a role in this subjective feeling, as it may do in actual orgasm.

definitions: (linguistics, of a word) Having the stress on the last syllable.

definitions: The absolute leader of a yakuza clan. He has an advisor called a saiko-komon.
(colloquial, by analogy) A person who keeps secrets.
The delicate oyster-shaped morsel of dark meat contained in a small cavity of the bone on each side of the lower part of the back of a fowl.
(UK, slang) Ellipsis of Oyster card.

Any of certain marine bivalve mollusks, especially those of the family Ostreidae (the true oysters), usually found adhering to rocks or other fixed objects in shallow water along the seacoasts, or in brackish water in the mouth of rivers.
A pale beige color tinted with grey or pink, like that of an oyster.
(UK, slang) A shoplifter.
(figurative) Something at one's disposal.
examples: Cut remaining skin on back. Remove the oyster (choice dark meat in spoon-shaped bone on back) with the leg.

Hard and sharp as flint, from which no steel had ever struck out generous fire; secret, and self-contained, and solitary as an oyster.

definitions: To fish for oysters.
examples: Most Fishneck watermen oystered in winter, using the same small skiffs from which they potted crabs in summer. Sometimes two men oystered from the same boat or took along a son or younger brother to cull oysters (separate those of legal size from undersized ones and shell).

definitions: Of a pale beige colour tinted with grey or pink, like that of an oyster.
Any of several black or pied coastal wading birds in the genus Haematopus that have a long red or orange bill and feed on shellfish.
examples: [T]he sea-swallows uttered their ringing "Tree, tree," and the oyster-catchers their mocking "Click, click!" which has caused so many an unsuccessful sportsman to smile.

definitions: tautog (Tautoga onitis)
plural of oyster
Any of the beetles in genus Ozaena, with one species, Ozaena dentipes.
(mineralogy) A dark waxy mineral, found associated with petroleum in some sandstones, used to make polishes.
(inorganic chemistry) An allotrope of oxygen (symbol O₃) having three atoms in the molecule instead of the usual two; it is a toxic gas, generated from oxygen by electrical discharge.
(British, informal) Fresh air, especially that breathed at the seaside and smelling of seaweed.
examples: It's got the lot: fresh sea air, ozone, seaweed. You could cut the air with a knife.

To Ramsgate baths she sped, in quest / Of seaweed and ozone; / For seaweed and ozone were best, / They said, to give her tone.

A patent obtained in England, and specified far from clearly, for obtaining ozone by boiling seaweed,†† may be mentioned as a curiosity, and also the credulity with which ozone -baths, prepared in this manner, find a ready sale, in spite of, or perhaps rather on account of, their high price.

definitions: To treat with ozone.
examples: I worked nonstop to make the house safe. Periodically I ozoned the first-floor bathroom, but it still made us sick.
Whenever it exists, as it usually does, even where the tide water freshens at the ebb, it seems to have a purifying tendency, probably by ozoning the superincumbent atmosphere.

definitions: (chemistry) the univalent anion, O 3, derived from ozone
(chemistry) any of a number of explosive organic compounds containing a -O-O-O- group

(chemistry) any of a class of heterocycles, containing two carbon atoms (in positions 3 and 5) and three oxygen atoms (in positions 1, 2 and 4) produced by the reaction of ozone with a carbon to carbon double bond; the 1,2,4- trioxolanes
(chemistry) any dark red salt of this anion and a metal
(mycology) A carpet of hyphae, resembling an above-ground mycelium, associated with certain mushrooms of the genus Coprinellus.
To convert oxygen into ozone, especially by using an ozonizer
To treat or react with ozone; to ozonate
A layer in part of the stratosphere that has a larger than normal concentration of ozone; the ozone layer.
examples: Coordinate terms: mesosphere, thermosphere

The mesosphere may be described as the earth's intermediate or middle atmosphere, which separates the thermosphere above from the ozonosphere (or stratosphere) below, both of which are responsible for absorption of most of the ultraviolet part of the solar radiation and preventing it from reaching the earth's surface.
